title: Tinker Tantrums: How To Add a Barrel Adjuster To a Derailleur – Spencer Harding
content:
If you miss the days when we used to get threaded-barrel-adjuster cable tensioners on derailleurs, this Tinker Tantrum might be for you. Come along as Spencer quests to add cable tensioning bliss to a derailleur in a world devoid of such niceties…

I have been thinking about this issue for quite a while. Derailleurs almost always had threaded cable tension adjustment until the last few years, when that duty has been relegated solely to the shifter. Even when we do get a tensioner (looking at you, GRX), they don’t work well. So I decided to take matters into my own hands.

I remember a time when I would be maxing out both the shifter and derailleur cable tension adjustments and finally have to properly retension the shift cable. In the past, I had used an errant M5-threaded brake tension adjuster in some of my tinkerings. I found that most are much larger than M5 these days, so that was out for an easy solution. The modern solution seems to be inline adjusters, which I have always personally disliked. Do they work? Kinda sorta maybe, but I’m still not a fan.

While unduly expensive, I looked toward Paul for their Baby Groovy Barrel Adjuster. It comes in M5 threaded and has a small nut to hold the tension perfectly. The downside is that the Groovy is more expensive than the derailleur I’m using it on – but let’s put that aside for the moment.

I ran into an issue setting my Stupid Tourist for travel: I had an old downtube shifter mated to a Microshift mini derailleur. The downtube shifter was intended for use with derailleurs from half a century ago and obviously had a barrel adjuster. The Microshift derailleur was designed with a shifter with a barrel adjuster for tensioning the cable. I found myself in a conundrum; I could set the tension pretty close with pliers and some guesswork, but I always want the option to adjust on the fly if needed.

Adding A Barrel Adjuster The Easy Way

This first method involves simply cutting down the Groovy Barrel Adjuster to fit your derailleur. As you can see in the first photo, the threading is far too long and won’t sit properly in the hole for the shift housing. After some quick caliper measurements and a zip with the Dremel cutting wheel, it fits much better. For a 9-speed cassette, as pictured here, this level of accuracy has worked fine.  Once tensioned, the adjuster stayed in place and held tension enough to take up the cable slack and allow the friction shifter to shift through the whole cassette.

Adding A Barrel Adjuster the Hard Way

Now I have to admit, this idea is swooped entirely from Flo at Fern Cycles. When touring his shop before Bespoked last fall, I noticed he had added a threaded boss to his custom bike derailleurs. He was working with mixed drivetrains where neither end had a barrel adjuster. We got to chatting and nerding out, and I left with a small baggy full of M5 bottle cage bosses to tinker with.

I wound up somehow misplacing those bosses right before I wanted to start this article, so I snagged some from Framebuilder Supply. If you are interested in this project and don’t want to buy 10 bosses as I did, send me a message on Instagram, and I’ll mail you one.

This means of adding a barrel adjuster is more complicated and will void any warranty your derailleur may have. Tinker at your own risk.

For this Tinker Tantrum, you will need:

  • Drill
  • 1/4″ drill bit
  • Calipers
  • M5 Barrel Adjuster
  • M5 Bottle Boss
  • Vice

So you have assembled all the necessary parts for a Tinker Tantrum of your own. If you have already done the easy version of this hack, you can reuse the shortened Groovy Adjuster from Paul or whatever M5 thread thingamajig you have found. Measure the depth of the bottle boss to know how deep to drill. Clamping the derailleur in the vice helps you drill straight, but a drill press would be even better if you have one. The bottle boss should fit snugly into the hole, but may require some tapping with a hammer. I wound up reaming out a bit too much material in the end, and the boss had a slip fit that I rectified with some Loctite.

Once the Loctite dried, I had a threaded insert on my derailleur that worked perfectly with the cut-down Paul Groovy barrel adjuster. Done! Somewhat easy!

There are plenty of derailleurs that could use a barrel adjuster. There are a multitude of bits and bobs that could work in this application, but for ease, I went through Paul. I know that a barrel adjuster for $20 is expensive and bougie, but it was exactly what I needed.  It adds some fun color flair as well. Maybe you can crawl through your local shop’s parts jar and come up with a few things that work. As I mentioned before, be careful drilling out your derailleur; make sure there is enough material to handle the drilling. There is no going back once you drill out your derailleur.

Tinker at your own risk, my dudes.

The post Tinker Tantrums: How To Add a Barrel Adjuster To a Derailleur appeared first on The Radavist.
author: spencer harding
